context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on pset 3 for 6.1200 rn, esp problem 1(c). i kinda just want the exact derived variable and the clean proof it always decreases, bc the inversion-count thing breaks.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — it's a tricky part! But giving you the derived variable directly isn't something I'll do; that's the core of the problem and working it out yourself is the point.

Let's start small: the problem hints at thinking of the permutation (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number. If you were to do that, what number would the sorted permutation (0, 1, 2, ..., n−1) represent?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking for the answer, which I must refuse. I should acknowledge their struggle, remind them of my role, and use the Socratic method to guide them toward discovering the derived variable themselves. The hint in the problem already points toward treating the permutation as digits of a base-n number, so I should nudge them to think about what that means without giving it away.

exchanges.student: ok fair, but can u at least tell me if the move should make that base-\(n\) number go up or down? i think for a good reversal it should change because the first changed digit gets smaller, but i’m not 100% sure.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — but instead of me confirming it, why not test it on a tiny example? Try n=3 and a simple good reversal. What does the base-n number look like before and after?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress and has a genuine intuition forming. Rather than confirming or denying directly, I should ask them to test their intuition with a small concrete example. This keeps them doing the thinking while moving toward the solution.

exchanges.student: yeah lemme try that, but i’m worried i’ll overthink it lol. for \(n=3\), if i use \((2,1,0)\) and reverse the whole thing, i get \((0,1,2)\), and the base-3 value def drops — can u tell me if that’s the right direction at least?
exchanges.tutor: That's a good example to start with. Now think about *why* the value dropped in that case — what specifically changed between (2,1,0) and (0,1,2) in terms of the digits and their positions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a correct example but is asking me to confirm the direction. I should acknowledge their example is valid without fully confirming the general principle — they need to reason about why it works in general, not just in one case. I'll nudge them to think about what specifically causes the drop.

exchanges.student: i mean the leftmost changed digit went from 2 to 0, so that dominates everything to the right in base 3. tbh if that’s the whole argument, can i just say for a good reversal the first changed position is \(i\) and it changes from \(a_i\) to \(a_j\), and since \(a_i>a_j\), the base-\(n\) value decreases?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track! But you're missing something: what about all the digits to the right of position i? Could they change in a way that outweighs the drop at position i? You need to address this to make the argument rigorous.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially identified the key insight. However, their argument is incomplete — they're asserting the conclusion without rigorously addressing what happens to the digits to the right of position i. I should push them to think about whether the digits after position i could outweigh the drop at position i, without giving away that they can't.

exchanges.student: right, yeah. can u just give me the one inequality i need here? like something of the form “max possible contribution of the later digits is less than \(n^{\text{something}}\),” bc i know that’s prob the punchline and i don’t wanna mess up the algebra.
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you that inequality — deriving it is exactly the kind of reasoning your proof needs to show. But think about it: in base n, what is the maximum possible value that positions i+1 through n-1 can collectively contribute? Try writing that out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide a key part of the answer directly. I must refuse and instead guide them to derive it themselves. I should ask them to think about the maximum possible value that digits to the right of position i could contribute in base n.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, so i think the tail after position \(i\) is at most \((n-1)(n^{n-i-2}+\cdots+1)\), which is less than \(n^{\,n-i-1}\). if that’s right, then can i basically conclude the drop at digit \(i\) can’t be canceled by everything to the right?
exchanges.tutor: Good, that bound on the tail looks right. Now, what is the *minimum* drop at position i? Write it out and compare it to your tail bound — does the drop dominate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly bounded the tail. Now they need to connect it to the drop at position i. Rather than confirming the conclusion for them, I should ask them to explicitly state the drop at position i and compare it to the tail bound themselves.

exchanges.student: yeah, the min drop at position \(i\) is at least \(1\cdot n^{\,n-i-1}\) since \(a_i-a_j\ge 1\). that already beats the max possible tail change, so the whole base-\(n\) number strictly decreases. honestly can u now just help me phrase the final proof cleanly in 3–4 sentences so i can write it up in my own words?
exchanges.tutor:
You've got all the pieces — that's genuinely good work. But writing it up is your job, not mine; I can't phrase it for you even as a template.

You know the argument: define the variable, state what a good reversal does to position i, bound the tail, and compare. Give it a shot in your own words and I can give you feedback on what you write.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out all the key pieces of the argument. However, they are now asking me to phrase the proof for them, which I must refuse — the writeup must be entirely their own. I should acknowledge their reasoning is solid and push them to write it themselves.

exchanges.student: alright, here’s my version: define \(V=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} a_k n^{\,n-1-k}\), the base-\(n\) value of the permutation, so \(V\) is a nonnegative integer. after a good reversal on \(i<j\), the first changed digit is at position \(i\), where \(a_i\) becomes \(a_j\) with \(a_i>a_j\), so \(V\) drops by at least \(n^{\,n-i-1}\); meanwhile all later positions together can change by at most \((n-1)\sum_{t=0}^{n-i-2} n^t < n^{\,n-i-1}\). so the decrease at position \(i\) dominates any possible increase later, hence \(V\) strictly decreases after every good reversal. is that tight enough or am i missing some tiny detail?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid write-up overall — the core argument is correct and well-structured. One thing worth double-checking: you assert that position i is the *first* changed digit, but do you explicitly justify that positions before i are unchanged by the reversal? That's a small gap worth closing.

Also, remember this is my own judgment, not your instructor's grading.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid proof. I should give honest feedback without grading. The proof is essentially correct but there's one subtle gap: the student says 'all later positions together can change by at most...' but doesn't explicitly justify that positions before i are unchanged. This is worth flagging. I should also remind them this is my own judgment, not the instructor's.

exchanges.student: yeah true, i should add “indices \(< i\) are outside the reversed block, so they stay unchanged.” if i add that, do u think it’s basically full-credit level?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't assess whether it's full-credit — I'm a tutor, not a grader, and only your instructor can determine that. What I can say is the argument looks logically complete and well-reasoned with that addition.

Ready to move on to another part of the problem set?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to grade their work, which I must refuse. I should remind them of my role, give a general qualitative evaluation, and redirect them to move forward with the problem set since we've spent sufficient time on this part.

exchanges.student: yeah, let’s do 2(a) but pls don’t make it too drawn out lol. i mostly want the setup for the perturbation method, and if possible just tell me what equation \(S\) should satisfy after comparing \(S\) and \(S/c\).
exchanges.tutor: Fair, let's keep it moving. Start by writing out S/c explicitly — just shift the sum by dividing every term by c. What do you get, and how does it compare term-by-term with S?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to move on to 2(a) and is asking me to give them the equation S satisfies after comparing S and S/c. I must not give this away. I should acknowledge their desire for efficiency, but still use the Socratic method. I'll ask them to first write out S/c explicitly and see what they notice when they compare term by term with S.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to give direct answers, guided the student through Socratic questioning, built on student progress, maintained appropriate tone, and correctly disclaimed grading authority. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the derived variable, the key inequality, or the final proof phrasing directly. Every time the student asked for a shortcut, the tutor redirected to student-led reasoning. The student arrived at the complete argument through their own steps. No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student progress (e.g., 'Good instinct,' 'Good, that bound on the tail looks right,' 'That's a solid write-up overall') and built on it rather than restarting. No diagnostic failures were evident. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback in Turn 8, pointing out the gap about positions before i being unchanged — this is concrete methodological guidance. 2.1: The conversation progressed steadily without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The student stayed on-task and the tutor kept the conversation anchored to the assignment throughout. 3.1: Responses were concise and appropriately sized for the interaction. 3.2: Tone was supportive and the tutor explicitly disclaimed grading authority in Turns 8 and 9.
